Integration Guardrails are more than just Integration Patterns – it's more than just Integration Architecture as well. Guardrails are defining the “boundaries" we follow when “integrating everything with everyone”. Architecture is just one “piece” of the puzzle – but this puzzle has many pieces.  

Conclusion

The main purpose of this article is to:

  • Connect the “dots” from the past (previous articles and terminology) with new “simplified” view and (high-level) patterns.
  • Explain that we can leverage from the past experiences and continue with our SAP Integration Suite in future – using of course Advance Event Mesh much more, but not neglecting the usage of Cloud Integration and API-M, as it all “fits” our future needs.

Can we build "fully" decoupled and loosely-coupled integrations around ISA-M with SAP Integration Suite in (near) future? What will SAP Business Data Cloud bring as per “new” Integration Patterns around Data Products? And what are AI and MCP really bringing in the integration Patterns?

Many questions, but future is not “certain” always. Maybe in few years (or less) I will be refreshing the Integration Patterns article with some new “buzz-word”…

Acknowledgment

*) Intro photo by Ales Krivec on Unsplash

References

[1] SAP Blog: Integration patterns for the Composable Business A... - SAP Community

[2] SAP Blog: What is Composable Architecture? - SAP Community

[3] Gartner: The Future of Business Is Composable - Gartner Keynote

[4] The OpenGroup: Boundaryless Information Flow™ | www.opengroup.org

[5] SAP ISA-M: Integration Methodology | Services and Support

[6] SAP CPI/API-M: SAP Integration Suite | Integration Software

[7] SAP AEM: Advanced Event Mesh | SAP Integration Suite

[8] SAP SLT: SAP Landscape Transformation Replication Server | SAP

entry.title: SAP Integration Suite Licensing: A Practical Guide for Businesses and Architects
entry.content:
Introduction

Within the SAP ecosystem, developers benefit from extensive documentation and technical tutorials. However, business leaders, enterprise architects, procurement teams, and startups often struggle to find clear guidance on licensing, pricing, and commercial models—particularly for SAP Integration Suite.

This blog bridges that gap by offering a practical, business-focused overview of SAP Integration Suite licensing, along with real-world case studies that illustrate how organizations can select the most suitable commercial model.

Why Licensing Information Is Hard to Find

Unlike technical documentation, commercial details are not widely publicized. Key reasons include:

1. Tailored Pricing Models

SAP solutions are customised based on:

  • Organization size and industry
  • Integration volumes and complexity
  • Geographic presence
  • Compliance requirements
  • Existing SAP investments

2. Sales-Led Engagement Model

Licensing information is typically provided through:

  • SAP account executives
  • Authorized SAP partners
  • Commercial proposals and Statements of Work

3. Consumption-Based Cloud Economics

SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) follows a consumption-based model where costs depend on usage metrics such as:

  • Integration runtime capacity
  • Messages processed
  • API calls and events
  • Compute and memory utilization
  • Additional capabilities like B2B Integration and API Management

4. Rapid Evolution of SAP Offerings

SAP frequently enhances its cloud offerings, making static pricing references less reliable over time.

Overview of SAP Integration Suite Commercial Models

1. Free Tier

Best For: Developers, students, and innovation teams

Purpose: Learning, experimentation, and prototyping

Benefits:

  • No cost
  • Ideal for proofs of concept
  • Hands-on experience

Limitations:

  • Restricted resources
  • Not intended for production use

2. Pay-As-You-Go (PAYG)

Best For: Startups, SMEs, and organizations with unpredictable workloads

Benefits:

  • No upfront commitment
  • Pay only for what you consume
  • Rapid scalability and flexibility

Considerations:

  • Requires cost governance
  • Expenses increase with scale

3. Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement (CPEA)

Best For: Large enterprises adopting multiple SAP BTP services

Benefits:

  • Prepaid cloud credits
  • Flexibility across SAP BTP services
  • Volume-based discounts
  • Centralized governance

Considerations:

  • Requires upfront financial commitment
  • Needs consumption planning

4. Subscription-Based Licensing

Best For: Organizations with stable and predictable workloads

Benefits:

  • Predictable costs
  • Simplified budgeting
  • Guaranteed capacity

Considerations:

  • Less flexibility compared to consumption models
  • Potential risk of underutilization

5. RISE with SAP

Best For: Organizations adopting SAP S/4HANA Cloud as part of digital transformation

Benefits:

  • Bundled services under a single contract
  • Simplified vendor management
  • Accelerated cloud adoption

Considerations:

  • Less granular pricing visibility
  • Best suited for SAP-centric landscapes

Decision Matrix: Choosing the Right Model

Business Scenario

Recommended Model

Learning and experimentation

Free Tier

Startups building their first integrations

Pay-As-You-Go

SMEs with growing integration needs

PAYG transitioning to CPEA

Enterprises adopting multiple BTP services

CPEA

Predictable, high-volume workloads

Subscription-Based Licensing

S/4HANA Cloud transformation initiatives

RISE with SAP

System integrators and consulting firms

PAYG for projects; CPEA for internal use

Key Cost Drivers for SAP Integration Suite

Cost Driver

Business Impact

Number of integrations

Influences runtime capacity

Message volume

Major contributor to consumption costs

API calls

Impacts API Management usage

B2B/EDI capabilities

Adds specialized costs

Number of environments (Dev, QA, Prod)

Increases consumption

Data transformation complexity

Affects processing requirements

High availability and scalability

Raises infrastructure costs

Security and compliance requirements

May increase operational overhead

Real-World Case Studies

Case Study 1: Startup Integrating SaaS Applications

Industry: HR Technology Startup

Challenge: Integrate a cloud-based HR platform with SAP S/4HANA for clients.

Solution: Pay-As-You-Go model.

Implementation:

  • Connected Salesforce, SAP S/4HANA, and SuccessFactors.
  • Leveraged API Management and Cloud Integration.
  • Scaled gradually based on customer demand.

Outcome:

  • Zero upfront investment.
  • Faster time-to-market.
  • Controlled operational expenses.

Why PAYG Was Ideal:

  • Unpredictable workloads.
  • Need for agility and cost efficiency.

Case Study 2: Manufacturing Enterprise Modernizing Legacy Systems

Industry: Global Manufacturing

Challenge: Replace point-to-point integrations across SAP ECC, Ariba, and third-party logistics providers.

Solution: Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement (CPEA).

Implementation:

  • Standardized enterprise integrations using SAP Integration Suite.
  • Enabled EDI and API-based partner connectivity.
  • Utilized BTP credits across multiple services.

Outcome:

  • Reduced integration complexity.
  • Improved supply chain visibility.
  • Optimized costs through prepaid credits.

Why CPEA Was Ideal:

  • Large-scale, enterprise-wide adoption.
  • Need for flexibility across multiple BTP services.

Case Study 3: Retail Chain Managing Seasonal Demand

Industry: Retail and E-Commerce

Challenge: Handle fluctuating integration volumes during festive and seasonal peaks.

Solution: Pay-As-You-Go model.

Implementation:

  • Integrated SAP Commerce Cloud with logistics and payment gateways.
  • Scaled integrations during peak sales periods.

Outcome:

  • Cost optimization during off-peak seasons.
  • Elastic scalability during high demand.

Why PAYG Was Ideal:

  • Variable workloads and seasonal spikes.

Case Study 4: Banking Institution Requiring Predictable Costs

Industry: Banking and Financial Services

Challenge: Ensure secure, compliant, and stable integrations across core banking systems.

Solution: Subscription-Based Licensing.

Implementation:

  • Integrated SAP and non-SAP systems with strict governance.
  • Ensured regulatory compliance and high availability.

Outcome:

  • Predictable budgeting and financial planning.
  • Stable, mission-critical operations.

Why Subscription Was Ideal:

  • Consistent transaction volumes.
  • Strong compliance requirements.

Case Study 5: Enterprise Migrating to SAP S/4HANA Cloud

Industry: Professional Services

Challenge: Transform legacy systems while migrating to SAP S/4HANA Cloud.

Solution: RISE with SAP.

Implementation:

  • Leveraged pre-packaged integrations and clean-core principles.
  • Enabled seamless connectivity across cloud applications.

Outcome:

  • Simplified vendor and contract management.
  • Accelerated digital transformation.

Why RISE with SAP Was Ideal:

  • Unified approach to ERP modernization and integration.

A Typical Adoption Journey

Most organizations follow this path:

Free Tier → Pay-As-You-Go → CPEA or Subscription → RISE with SAP

This approach enables businesses to:

  • Validate use cases
  • Optimize investments
  • Scale efficiently
  • Reduce financial risks

Essential Questions to Ask SAP or Partners

Before selecting a commercial model, consider asking:

  1. What metrics determine Integration Suite costs?
  2. Which capabilities are included in the license?
  3. How are non-production environments billed?
  4. Are there volume discounts available?
  5. What governance tools exist for cost monitoring?
  6. Can the commercial model be converted later?
  7. What is the estimated total cost of ownership over three years?
  8. How does pricing scale as the organization grows?

Recommended Resources

  • SAP Discovery Center 
  • SAP BTP Pricing Calculator
  • SAP Help Portal
  • SAP Community Blogs and Q&A
  • SAP Learning Hub and openSAP
  • Authorized SAP Partners and Account Executives

Key Takeaways

  • Pay-As-You-Go is a licensing model—not a license-free option.
  • CPEA provides flexibility and cost optimization for enterprises.
  • Subscription models offer predictable costs for stable workloads.
  • RISE with SAP simplifies ERP-led transformations.
  • Architects must align technical architecture with commercial strategy.
  • Understanding licensing early prevents costly redesigns later.

Conclusion

While developers benefit from extensive technical guidance, businesses and architects often lack clarity around licensing and pricing. By understanding SAP Integration Suite’s commercial models, organizations can make informed decisions that align technology investments with business goals.

Selecting the right licensing model ensures scalability, cost efficiency, and long-term success in the cloud integration journey.

Call to Action

Have you implemented SAP Integration Suite using PAYG, CPEA, Subscription, or RISE with SAP? Share your experiences and insights in the comments to help the SAP Community learn and grow together.

Thank you for reading!

Connect in LinkedIn : Naveen Kumar 

Disclaimer:
This article reflects personal insights and is intended for educational and informational purposes only. It does not represent the official position of SAP. Pricing, licensing, and commercial models are subject to change and may vary by region and customer agreement. For the most accurate and up-to-date information, please consult SAP directly or an authorized SAP partner. SAP and other SAP products mentioned herein are trademarks or registered trademarks of SAP SE in Germany and other countries.

entry.title: Clean Core by Design: Event Enablement Cockpit as a Core Integration Enabler in SAP S/4HANA
entry.content:
Enterprise Event Enablement is one of the core components of the modern SAP Enterprise Integration Architecture for SAP S/4 HANA, which enables seamless exchange of business events across different platforms for Event-Driven communication. Events from SAP S/4 can be sent to SAP BTP Integration Suite Event Mesh/ AEM/ SAP Cloud Application Event Hub, where they can be consumed by any recipient based on the requirement.
To exchange events between SAP S/4 and  BTP EM, a communication connection setup is required. This connection is maintained by the Enterprise Event Enablement framework, which enables the data flow from the SAP S/4 HANA to the Business Event Consumer, and BTP services play it's role as the message broker(Integration Suite EM/ AEM).

A channel represents a single connection to a service instance of the SAP BTP, Event Mesh. For more information, see Creating, Configuring and Managing Channels.

Note: Channel configurations can be done via SAP Event Mesh, SAP Advance Event Mesh or SAP Cloud Application Event Hub, depending on your scenario and architecture, in this blog we will be going through SAP EM. For SAP ECC, you need ASAPIO for Events Configuration/ Enablement because Standard Business Events, like they are natively available in S/4 are not available in ECC.



SAP S/4 Event Producers

Non SAP S/4 Event Producers

Create a Destination through the SM59 transaction and setup the OAuth 2.0 configuration on the S/4 HANA system, both must be configured as per the service key instance of the corresponding event mesh instance.
After the channel creation is completed, you can list which events can be part of this channel, and an outbound binding needs to be setup to publish the events of an S/4 HANA system.
All administration, configuration, and monitoring steps are available in transaction SPRO under the Enterprise Event Enablement Node,

Under the Enterprise Event Enablement framework, below are the authorization objects and roles that are available,
Under the Hood of Enterprise Event Enablement Framework, the available authorization objects and roles are below,

Authorization Objects: /IWXBE/ADM, /IWXBE/USR, /IWXBE/SUP

Roles: SAP_IWXBE_RT_XBE_ADM, SAP_IWXBE_RT_XBE_BUSI, SAP_IWXBE_RT_XBE_MDT

Creating, Configuring and Managing Channels

  1. Create an EM Service Instance
  2. Creating Channels using a Service Key
    1. Creating and Managing Channels
      1. Create an RFC Destination
      2. OAuth 2.0 Client Config
      3. Manage Channel and Parameters

1. Create an EM Service Instance
Create a service instance for the EM,

  1. Go to your SAP BTP cockpit global account and subaccount.
      - To see how the navigation works in the SAP BTP cockpit, refer to Navigate in the Cockpit.
  2. Expand Services  Instances and Subscriptions.
  3. Choose Create.
  4. Choose Event Mesh and the default service plan.


  5. Choose your Cloud Foundry space, enter an instance name, and choose Next.

  6. Specify parameters using a JSON file for the Service Key.
    Example:
{
  "emname": "<emname>",
  "namespace": "<namespace e.g. a/b/c>",
  "version": "1.1.0",
  "options": {
    "management": true,
    "messagingrest": true,
    "messaging": true
  },
  "rules": {
    "queueRules": {
        "publishFilter": [
            "${namespace}/*"
        ],
        "subscribeFilter": [
            "${namespace}/*"
        ]
    },
    "topicRules": {
        "publishFilter": [
            "${namespace}/*"
        ],
        "subscribeFilter": [
            "${namespace}/*"
        ]
    }
  },
	"xs-security": { //optional//
     "oauth2-configuration": { 
      "credential-types": [
        "binding-secret",
        "x509"
      ]
    }
  }
}

2. Creating Channels using a Service Key
Once the service instance key is created, it will have all the necessary information to make a connection, it will be used in the automatic process creation and configuration of the destination and OAuth 2.0 config, which will be handled by the Event Enablement Framework itself.

To create a channel using a service key, proceed as follows:
1. Execute the Event Enablement Configuration.
2. Press the via Service Key (Create new channel via Service Key) button.


3. Enter the following details:
    - a. Channel
    - b. Description
    - c. Destination (Enter your preferred RFC destination name)
    - d. OAuth 2.0 Configuration (Enter your preferred name. The name must not have been used yet)
4. Enter the service key.

5. Choose Save configuration or press Enter.

Choose Save configuration or press Enter.

2.i. Creating and Managing Channels

2.i.a. Creating RFC destination,
Steps:
1. Go to BTP -> Event Mesh -> Cloud Foundry Environment, to get the certificates, follow the steps below:
     - Launch the token endpoint URL, https://<enterprise messaging url>/oauth/token that you receive from the service instance of SAP EM.
     - Export all certificates from the certificates path.
2. Logon to the SAP S/4HANA, on-premise system that has the SAP_GWFND component.
3. Open the transaction STRUST, select the required node SSL Client (Standard) and import all the certificates.
4. Open the transaction SMICM, select Administration ICM Exit Soft Local to clear the cache.
5. In SPRO, open SAP Reference IMG and navigate to SAP Customizing Implementation Guide SAP NetWeaver Enterprise Event Enablement Administration Channel Connection Settings.
6. Open Manage RFC Destination.
7. Create the RFC destination, give name and description and select the connection type as G and Save.
8. Under Technical Settings, in Target System Setting, in the Host field, enter the domain name of the <app-url> given in the URI, example, wss://<app-url>/protocols/mqtt311ws of the service instance key in the mqtt311ws protocol.
9. (Optional) Under Technical Settings, in HTTP Proxy Settings, enter the proxy details.
10. Under the Logon and Security tab, in the Security option, enable SSL as Active and import the SSL Certificate from the certificate list and save.

2.i.b. OAuth 2.0 Client Config

Steps:

1. Inside SPRO, open the SAP Reference IMG and navigate to SAP Customizing Implementation Guide SAP NetWeaver Enterprise Event Enablement Administration Channel Connection Settings.
2. Open the Manage OAuth Client Setup and Click on Create.
3. In OAuth 2.0 Client Profile field, select the /IWXBE/MGW_MQTT profile and Enter a configuration name, and in Client ID, enter the EM service instance Client ID and press ok.
4. Under the Details section, choose the Administration and place the Client Secret you receive from the EM service instance.
5. Under Authorization Server Settings, enter the following:
   - In the Authorization Endpoint field, enter the token endpoint URL of the service instance and replace <token> with <authorize>.
   - For the Token Endpoint field, set the token endpoint URL of the service instance.
6. In the Access Settings, under the Resource Access Authentication section, choose Header Field.
7. Select the Grant Type as Client Credentials and enter the proxy settings(if applicable) and save.

 2.i.c. Manage Channel and Parameters

Steps:

1. Go to SPRO, open the SAP Reference IMG and navigate to SAP Customizing Implementation Guide  SAP NetWeaver  Enterprise Event Enablement  Administration  Channel Connection Settings  Manage Channels and Parameters or execute transaction.
2. Either create the new or select the already created one(based on the requirement).
3. Create/ Configure the output binding configuration,


4. In the Topic Name field, enter the namespace derived from the service key of the given instance(Topic space is the identifier for events that originate from the source)

5. Make the following entries in the Parameters section (mandatory).
- OAuth 2.0 Client Profile: /IWXBE/MGW_AMQP.
- OAuth 2.0 Configuration: Select the configuration name created in OAuth 2.0 Account Client Configuration.
6. Entries can be made in the optional parameters, below are the available parameters:
- Max Reconnect Attempts: Enterprise Event Enablement Framework executes to a defined number of times reestablish the connection in case of connection failure, the value of 0 or no value, the framework tries to connect until the connection is established. The channel is deactivated after reaching the reconnect attempts and still the connection is not established.
- Quality of Service (QoS): There are two values:
   - At Most Once(The message arrives at SAP BTP, EM mostly once)
   - At Least Once(QoS=1, default value if no value is entered).
     This quality of service ensures that the message arrives at SAP BTP, EM at least once)
- Reconnect Waittime:
- The Enterprise Event Enablement framework waits for the specified time(in seconds) and after that tries to reconnect. The framework is setup in such a way when the failure occurs, it increases the waittime till it reaches the Max Reconnect waittime(1800 seconds). The default reconnect waittime is 10 seconds, if the Max Reconnect Attempts value is not maintained, the framework tries to reconnect until the connection is established but the channel will not be deactivated during this period.
7. Choose the Save configuration button. or press Enter and select either Active<->Deactivate to activate or deactivate the channel.
- If the channel is active, only then will the events be sent. The channel might be in inactive status if the connection was terminated and reconnects failed.
- The channel will be deactivated during runtime, if the number of reconnect attempts are completed and the connection is still not established.
8. Press Check connection to perform a connection test.
9. If the connection test fails for an active channel, the corresponding error message is displayed. The same applies to an inactive channel.
 
 
Event Discovery Service Activation Step:
Once the Above steps are done, activate the Event Discovery Service and start Maintaining the Event Topics Accordingly,
Procedure

1. In transaction SPRO, open the SAP Reference IMG and navigate to Enterprise Event Enablement Administration.
2. Execute Activate Event Discovery Service and Choose Add Service, add alias as LOCAL and give External Service Name as BROWSER_SRV and then click on Get Services.
3. Select the service and Choose Add Selected Services, save it as local object and choose continue.
4. If the system alias is missing, go to System Aliases pane, choose Add System Alias and select new entries.
5. Enter the Service Doc Identifier as ZBROWSER_SRV_0001 and enter LOCAL in the SAP System Alias field.
6. Enable the Default System checkbox and click on save.
7. In the ICF Nodes pane, choose ICF Node and choose Activate.

 

 Configure the EM Client:

1. Message Clients
Goto EM Application and check the Message Clients( if case there is no, then create one otherwise you use the existing one)


2. Create Queue


3. Queue Subscription

4. Subscribe to the specific topics as per the requirement

5. Create a Webhook(Optional)
Use a webhook only when the consumer application does not support AMQP or MQTT protocols.

Conclusion



Now the setup is completed, try to test it through the test tab, and you are ready to enable the business events from the S/4 system. In this way, you will be able to set up the Event-Driven Architecture effectively within the S/4 system. Through this approach, you can set up a standardized and scalable way of exposing the Business Events in real time, enabling the flow of information through multiple ecosystems across the enterprise landscapes. By applying this approach, you can remove the reliance on the tightly coupled design and be more flexible through a loosely coupled design, which makes all the systems notified instantly.



References:
https://help.sap.com/docs/sap-cloud-application-event-hub/sap-cloud-application-event-hub-service-guide/integration-use-cases
https://help.sap.com/docs/abap-cloud/abap-integration-connectivity/creating-and-managing-channels?version=s4_hana
https://help.sap.com/docs/BTP/65de2977205c403bbc107264b8eccf4b/0874895f1f78459f9517da55a11ffebd.html
https://asapio.com/docs/connectors/sap-aem/
https://asapio.com/docs/designer/?asl_highlight=clean&p_asid=1

entry.title: Experience Modernized Enterprise Integration – Onsite!
entry.content:
After the latest wave of exciting announcements at SAP Sapphire 2026, you may still be buzzing from all the innovative technologies that lie ahead of us. But most of you probably want to see these innovations in action - interactive, hands-on, and with real people onsite.

Luckily, for everything related to Modernized Integration, we have something in store for you: the Integration Modernization AI Infodays in Germany. If you are based in Germany or located nearby, you can register for one of three onsite events and learn how to Accelerate Innovation with AI and Modern Integration.

Register here: https://events.sap.com/ai-driven-integration/en_us/home.html 

The Integration Modernization campaign turns inspiration into execution - showing how SAP Integration Suite operates as the control plane for Agent Governance: the backbone where APIs, events, identities, policies, and observability converge to safely orchestrate human and AI agents at scale.

We translate vision into actionable architecture by modernizing legacy middleware, establishing API- and event-first design principles, and operationalizing guardrails for AI and agent workloads.

Attendees will learn how to:

  • Understand new integration patterns such as Agent-to-System and Agent-to-Agent
  • Explain the MCP Reference Architecture
  • Use the MCP protocol to connect and govern agent capabilities in a policy-aware way
  • Easily consume APIs with consistent security, throttling, and lifecycle controls via SAP Integration Suite
  • Automate workflows (e.g., with n8n) to build event-driven, composable solutions - accelerating innovation while staying within enterprise guardrails
  • Understand identity setup across integration components (e.g., API keys and SAP IAS)

With SAP Integration Suite providing secure connectivity, policy enforcement, and end-to-end monitoring, you'll reduce technical debt, increase delivery velocity, and move decisively toward an autonomous enterprise.

After SAPPHIRE comes the opportunity to get your hands dirty - join us on the journey.

If you are interested please refer to this registration page to secure your spot: https://events.sap.com/ai-driven-integration/en_us/home.html

Stay tuned!

entry.title: Configuring OAuth Credentials for SAP SuccessFactors – A Step by Step Guide
entry.content:
Hello All,

Greetings for the Day!!

Basic Authentication is being phased out for SuccessFactors. If your standard or custom SAP Cloud Integration (CPI) interfaces still rely on Basic Authentication, now is the time to transition to OAuth2.0.

Even though the SAP documentation regarding OAuth2 SAML is comprehensive enough, this article will guide you practically through the setup. It covers the end-to-end configuration across both CPI and SuccessFactors, including generation of certificates and registering your credentials.

This article outlines the exact methodology I utilize during the project implementations. It emphasizes the proper sequence of operations within both systems to ensure you can activate OAuth seamlessly, eliminating the need for trial and error. The steps are straightforward, reproducible, and perfect for distributing among your project members.

Prerequisites

  • Access to your SAP CPI tenant with permissions for Monitor → Keystore and Security Material.
  • Administrator access in SuccessFactors to manage OAuth2 Client Applications
  • SuccessFactors Company ID along with API/Data Center URL
  • A dedicated technical/API user in SuccessFactors

Architecture Overview

From a high level perspective, here is how the OAuth flow functions:

  1. CPI creates a key pair (the certificate);
  2. The certificate is then registered in the SuccessFactors by setting up an OAuth2 client mapped to a technical user;
  3. SuccessFactors generates an API Key specific to this client application.
  4. In CPI, an OAuth2 SAML Bearer Assertion credential is configured using the client parameters;
  5. Lastly, the iFlow leverages this credential to obtain access tokens, enabling secure communication with SuccessFactors APIs.

Part A - CPI: Create the Key Pair (Steps 1 - 4)

Step 1: Log in to your SAP CPI environment and navigate to Monitor → Integrations and APIs → Key store.

Step 2: Click Create → Key Pair.

Step 3: Populate the Key Pair parameters:

  • Alias: Choose a recognizable name for your setup (e.g., successfactorsoAuth).
  • Common Name (CN): Enter the exact SuccessFactors technical user ID.
  • Country/Region: Input your location code, such as IN (India).
  • Validity: set a sufficient validity period (don’t keep default short dates)

Step 4: Hit Create, then choose Download Certificate. You will need this certificate for the SuccessFactors configuration.

Tip: Keep a secure record of both the Alias and the CN, as you will need to reference them later during the CPI OAuth setup.

Part B - Success Factors: Register the OAuth Client (Steps 5 - 9)

Step 5: Sign into Success Factors and use the search tool to find Manage OAuth2 Client Applications.

Step 6: Click Register Client Application.

 

 

 

 

Step 7: Enter application details:

  • Application Name: You can give any meaningful name for your architechture.
  • Application URL: your middleware base URL (up to *.ondemand.com)
  • Check the box for Bind to Users
  • In User IDs section, provide the SF technical user (this must exactly match the CN you used in CPI)

Now, paste the CPI certificate:

  1. Open the downloaded certificate file using a basic text editor (like Notepad).
  2. Copy only the text situated between
    -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE----- and -----END CERTIFICATE-----
  3. Paste this string into X.509 Certificate field.

Click Register.

Step 8: Select View next to the newly created application to see its details.

Step 9: Copy the generated API Key and store it safely. This will be required for the CPI configuration.

Common pitfall: If the certificate block includes the BEGIN/END CERTIFICATE lines or extra white space, registration can fail. Only paste the primary block of text.

Part C - CPI: Create OAuth2 SAML Bearer Assertion Credential (Steps 10 - 12)

Step 10: Return to CPI and navigate to Monitor → Integrations and APIs → Security Material.

Step 11: Go to Create → OAuth2 SAML Bearer Assertion.

Step 12: Create a new entry with the following details:

  • Name: An identifiable title (e.g., SF_OAuth_SAML_Prod)
  • Audience: www.successfactors.com
  • Token Service URL: your API host + /oauth/token
    (For instance: https://api4.successfactors.com/oauth/token - make sure use your specific Data Center host).
  • Target System Type: Select SuccessFactors.
  • Company ID: your SF Company ID.
  • User ID: Select Key Pair Common Name (CN).
  • Key Pair Alias: the alias you created (e.g., successfactorsoAuth).
  • Client Key: The API key you retrieved from SuccessFactors.
  • Deploy.

 

Notes: 

  • The User ID must match the user you bound in SuccessFactors.
  • The Key Pair Alias must be exactly the CPI keystore alias.
  • The Token Service URL must be your DC’s API endpoint with /oauth/token.

Part D - Test with a Simple iFlow (Steps 13 - 15)

Step 13: Build a basic test iFlow that calls a simple SuccessFactors API (e.g., a GET on FOCompany or User with minimal fields).

Step 14: In the receiver adaptor settings, apply the following:
Authentication: Set to OAuth2 SAML Bearer Assertion.
Select the credential you created above.

Step 15: Turn HTTP Session Reuse = On Exchange (important).
This minimizes token overhead and prevents intermittent auth failures.

Deploy and execute the test. You should receive a valid response from Success Factors.

Troubleshooting (what usually goes wrong)

401/403 errors:

  • Review your Audience and Token URL (spelling mistakes or incorrect DC hosts happen frequently).
  • Verify that HTTP Session Reuse is turned on in the adapter settings.
  • Double-check the Company ID and User ID in the CPI credential.

Invalid certificate / client errors:

  • Re‑paste only the certificate body exclusively (without BEGIN/END lines).
  • Check the CPI Keystore to make sure the Key Pair hasn't passed its expiration date.

Wrong user context:

  • The User ID applied in “Bind to Users” must be the same user used as CN in the CPI Key Pair and User ID in CPI OAuth credential.

Token URL not reachable:

  • Confirm if the API host is correct for your data center.
  • Ensure there are no proxies/firewalls that might be restricting outgoing connections from CPI to SF.

Best Practices (My Project Recommendations)

  1. Make use of Technical User in SF for API interactions rather than relying on personal or administrator accounts;
  2. Monitor certificate validity – set up calendar reminders 30–45 days before expiry.
  3. Store API Keys securely; refrain from sharing them in casual chats or open documents.
  4. Enable HTTP Session Reuse for the SuccessFactors adapters.
  5. Start with a minimal test iFlow before applying changes to your live business integrations.
  6. Review OAuth clients in SF quarterly; remove unused clients and rotate certs periodically.
  7. Use CPI Monitoring & Traces for root cause; they’re incredibly valuable for OAuth issues.

Conclusion

If you have followed the above mentioned instructions generating the key pair in CPI, OAuth client in SuccessFactors, credential in CPI, and a small test iFlow you’re set for a clean migration from Basic Auth to OAuth2 SAML Bearer Assertion.

The vast majority of errors I encounter are configuration typos or missed details (wrong token URL, incorrect User ID/CN mapping, certificate pasted incorrectly, or HTTP Session Reuse left off). Once those are correct, the flow is very stable.

Implementing this architecture ensures your integrations remain aligned with SAP’s roadmap, preventing you from unexpected disruptions when Basic Authentication is officially retired.

Best Regards,
Rasesh Thakkar.

entry.title: System API, Business API, Experience API – as a “governance” concept
entry.content:
Having your APIs into layers is not a new concept[1][2][3][4], but somehow overtime, we may have neglected it. The fact is, we turned design & implementation focus on specific technologies we used, not so much around “who does what”. But, applying API layers may still have many benefits, and it does not mean we need to change the integration technology we use[5].

Here I will try to show some fairly agnostic approach, using some SAP Integration Suite examples, but not limiting ourselves to any specific IPaaS or Event Broker.

To start with…

Yes, ISA-M (Integration Solution Advisory Methodology)[6] does not foster API layers – but is this limiting us in applying this concept with SAP tools?

To be honest no – SAP Integration Suite and its complete ecosystem (IPaaS, API-M, Advanced Event Mesh) are fully flexible set of tools, giving us all the flexibility we need to organize our APIs or EventAPIs the way we want. Publishing APIs via API Business Hub (or subsidiaries) is neither limiting nor contradicting the concept of API layers – this concept is not related with tools, nor even methodology we may use.

As already stated (in my previous article: Integration Guardrails are more than Integration Patterns of the Composable Business), we are defining our Integration Patterns, and Integration Guardrails. At the end it is on us; we define Integration Strategy we will use in the Organization/Enterprise; we define terminology, methodology and frameworks (or guardrails). Now, of course, in some cases it might be good to be very much ridged and apply certain best practices to the letter – but in some cases, we may apply common sense and choose what and how to tailor for our needs. Choosing why and what, how, or which part of what – is not an easy task, but this is why Organizations/Enterprises do have IT Architects. Please note, it’s not exactly “cherry picking”, although sometimes it might look just like that… In fact, tailoring our way is the foundation on which we build our integrations for the Intelligent Enterprise – and we do need to understand the complexity of our Organization/Enterprise, our needs, our aspirations and our maturity. Needless to say, how important it is, we do this right – from the day one…

But to go back to the main topic – yes, we can “add” API/EventAPI layers into our Integration Patterns – and no, this is not tool related (despite some terminology might lead us toward some specific IPaaS, other than SAP).

API layers

We do not “really” build things differently – there is no technical “splitting” or different tools we need to build this concept. This is all about logical split, organizing our APIs and our work per specific layers.

I.e. an Experience API of one Consumer may need to consume data one or more Business APIs, where each Business API may on its own be connected to one or more Providers and its System APIs,

Figure 1. What are API layers?

Logical rules are fairly simple:

  • one Experience APIs is connected to one or more Business API(s)
  • one Business API is connected to one or more System API(s), but in general terms Business API may also be connected to anther Business API(s)

Let’s talk through examples…

We start with simple Sync Request Reply integration pattern.

Figure 2. Simple API layers

What are the layers we have?

  • Provider, as a “system”, is exposing its own API (i.e. OData API), it may be standard or custom, no matter – in all cases we will just call it System API. This is an API provided by the system or application.
  • We will expose this API via API Gateway (i.e. implementing specific policies, throttling etc.), there might be some transformations (or value mappings) in the Cloud Integration before API Gateway – in all cases finally exposed API we will designate as Business API. This is an API we are providing to other Consumers based on the business requirements.
  •  Consumers need to be able to consume data (i.e. invoke HTTP Request, and receive HTTP Reply), and here as well we can have transformations (or value mappings) in the Cloud Integration in between – all this what we consume is called Experience API. This is an API which is adjusted to each Consumer.

Now, these “layers” are not strict technical and technology layers, but rather semantical and logical divisions – and this is critical to understand.

Of course this is a very simple a generic example. Let’s observe now a more realistic example of Sync Request Reply integration pattern, where Consumer(s) are creating new Sales Order in the Provider.

Figure 3. More complex APIs

Now we actually have several Providers:

  • We may have several SAP and non-SAP systems responsible for Order Fulfilment, where different systems are covering different Sales Organizations – i.e. we have non-SAP Provider (RESTful API), legacy SAP Provider (RFC call) and S/4HANA Provider (OData API). Each underlying system is exposing its own System API (although RFC is not “strictly” speaking an API, but we will be consuming it through Cloud Integration adapter).
  • Despite the fact Order Fulfilment is managed by different systems for different Sales Organizations, we want to provide seamless experience for all connecting systems when consuming this Business API. We will ensure routing policy within API Gateway, as well as necessary transformations within Cloud Integration – i.e. to transform message for non-SAP Provider or transform for legacy SAP Provider. From the perspective of consuming systems, this complexity is hidden – they will see only single API schema exposed to them.
  • Finally, each Consumer will consume data through its own Experience API. Please note, Experience API may be connected to several Business APIs (even though in this diagram only one Business API is shown).

What about EDA and EventAPIs?

But this concept does not end with “standard” Sync Request Reply integration pattern APIs. Yes, we can use it with PubSub or Async Request-Reply as well.

Figure 4. EventAPI layers

This example is with PubSub Integration Pattern.

  • Provider is publishing a business event to subscribed Consumer(s). In case of S/4HANA it can be standard CloudEvent, or RAP created event, or we may use Advanced Event Mesh add-on (ASAPIO) for building and publishing events – in all cases we call this System EventAPI. This is an EventAPI provided by the system or application.
  • An event message is published directly on the Advanced Event Mesh Topic, or optionally it may go through Cloud Integration (i.e. message payload adjustment). The Topic with its Schema Registry and any associated transformation (either in Cloud Integration or MI – Micro Integrations) is now representing Business EventAPI. Why Topic, why not Queue? Simple because Queue is actually a subscriber to the Topic, and it may subscribe only to subset of Topic messages (i.e. using dynamic elements) or it may subscribe to several Topics or both. It is the Topic which is providing EventAPI to other Consumers based on the business requirements.
  • Consumers may be subscribed directly to the Topic, or it may consume data through Queue, and in some cases additional transformations are needed between Queue and the Consumer – all this what we consume is called Experience EventAPI. This is now an EventAPI which is filtered and/or adjusted to each Consumer.

The proposed concept is “splitting” Advanced Event Mesh broker into two layers –part which belongs to Business EventAPI and part which belongs to Experience EventAPI. But rational is, I believe, quite strong – it is Topic and Queue what is decoupling Provider and Consumer, so it does make sense to assign broker operations into both layers – well, if we fully embrace this approach…

Worth noticing, with some other brokers this “splitting” may not work exactly like this. If we use Kafka broker we are using persisting Topics with multiple copies. But this is still not an issue. Applying the similar logic, each copy may be considered as part of the Experience EventAPI, right?

What about complex scenarios?  

In reality, integration flows are usually more complex. In this following example we will see that one Business API may invoke another Business API, In this example we will also see Experience API may be connected to more than one Business API.

Figure 5. Complex scenario with APIs and EventAPIs

In many cases we will have to combine APIs and EventAPIs. Here is one possible “complex” scenario:

  • Provider is publishing a business event. This is System EventAPI.
  • But this event might not have all necessary message data we need; it may serve only as a trigger to Cloud Integration for composing more complex message for the Business EventAPI. Cloud Integration will be requesting additional data from the other system through respective Business API and System API, and then publish full message to the Advanced Event Mesh Topic.
  • Also, the Consumer which is subscribed (either directly or via Queue) may expect further enhanced message data as part of its Experience EventAPI. Again, Cloud Integration will be acquiring additional data from the other system through respective Business API and System API.

In this example I did not cover all the possible complexities, as this is only a high-level concept document– but I believe the “picture” is clear…

Other Integration Patterns…

The same way we can do this concept with Sync Request-Reply, Async Request-Replay and PubSub integration patterns – no limitations to apply it with other integration patterns, as long as we have “things” decoupled or loosely coupled. Will not go into details, at least not in this article, as I believe “picture” is clear here as well…

It’s all about reusability, governance…

Why? Why should we introduce this concept? It’s not that we did not use all this in past, but we just did not make any “logical” separations…

I am not bringing any new technology or solution, I am talking about the concept – and now I need to explain its main benefits, otherwise, why should we bother to do it, right?

Reusability

From one side. we work in heterogeneous environments; connecting various Applications and Data Objects is usually not very straight forward activity. Using middleware is almost must, if we want to ensure some sort of decoupled or loosely coupled integrations (needed for the Intelligent Enterprise).

From the other side, we want to have our integrations managed, catalogued, and ready to be re-used by other Applications.

  • The thing is, each Provider has either standard or custom APIs (or EventAPIs). These System APIs (or EventAPIs) are tailored by the Provider system and its underlaying technology – and usually does not include Business needs or non-functional requirements (i.e. policies) of the particular Organization/Enterprise.
  • To build reusable APIs within Organization/Enterprise, we may have to take those System APIs, enhance it, transform it, or even group it into logical units which will be then exposed for further (re)use. By doing that, we are in fact building Business APIs (or EventAPIs).
  • Finally, we do build Business APIs to enable maximum reusability within the Organization/Enterprise, but in some cases, some Consumer(s) may not (technically) be able to consume those APIs without further adjusting to its own technology – so there might be an additional layer on top of Business APIs preparing data for the particular Consumer(s). This is now called Experience APIs (or EventAPIs)

Clearly, reusability is achieved by Business API, as here we build APIs which will meet the majority (if not all) Business needs and non-functional requirements of our Organization/Enterprise. We are fully decoupling Provider and Consumer(s), we do not adjust Provider toward each specific Consumer(s), nor do we adjust Consumer(s) to accept data from each Provider. Yet, in all cases we do have fully managed Business APIs (or EventAPIs) ready for every new Consumer to (re)use.

Governance

With Business APIs, we are bridging the complexity between Provider and Consumer(s), it’s built to be reused, but it’s built to be governed as well.

  • Provider is responsible for exposing its System APIs providing all necessary Business Data Objects and relevant statuses as per adjected processes. Provider is (normally) not aware how this data will be consumed by other Consumer(s).
  • Middleware is responsible for consuming System APIs, transforming it (per general need) and exposing it as Business APIs as reusable integration artifacts. Middleware is aware of the schemes and definitions of all relevant System APIs coming from Provider, as well as requirements for building general-purpose Business APIs ready for Consumer(s) to take and consume.
  • Consumer(s) are responsible for performing any individual adjustments and build for itself Experience APIs, for its own Business Data Objects and relevant statuses. In this process we may also need to use specific middleware components/tools. Consumer(s) are not aware how this data was provided by the Provider.

Figure 6. Split of responsibilities

By having this split, we can now easily “play” with specific governance rules and set rules of engagements of different Agile teams. I.e.:

  • Product Team, working on the specific Provider system, is responsible for exposing relevant data via System APIs, taking into consideration requirements of the overall Business Process. However, this team is not involved in the overall integration design process.
  • Integration Team, working on various middleware tools, is responsible for understanding both technology (i.e. System APIs technical details), but also overall requirements for the integration (i.e. what is it used for, by whom, how etc.). Obviously in its work Integration Team will apply specific Integration Guardrails and Guidelines. While it might sound a bit strange (to some), this is not only technical tasks but usually requires understanding of the underlaying Business Process. One does not have to be an expert at all, but knowing how to ask right questions is critical. This is why Integration Architecture cannot be just “coding” and “development” in middleware tools. The role of the Integration Architects is critical in the overall integration process, as they are solutioning this “bridge” between Provider and Consumer.
  • Product Team, working on the specific Consumer system, is responsible for building relevant Experience APIs, taking into consideration requirements and (possible?) constraints of its Application. However, this team is not involved in the overall integration design process. It is interesting to note here, that this Product Team may also using different integration IT Components (or even IPaaS) which are more tightly connected with the consuming Application (i.e. using Azure Service Bus with Azure Function for inbound/outbound traffic to/from Dynamics 365 might make perfect sense).

Figure 7. Move toward API layers

There are several (positive!) implications from this split of Roles & Responsibilities.

  1. Integration is everybody business – integrating two or more Applications is not a sole responsibility of the Integration Team.
  2. Governance is everybody concern – all participants must do their part as per prescribed governance rules of engagements, we all must “give” something in order to “get” something.
  3. Integration Architects design integration – people who have knowledge and skills are entrusted to do what they are meant to be doing.

As we know from experiences, “dropping” integration requirements or designs form the Product Owners or “other” IT Architects, was usually resulting in poorly governed integration flows, usually built to fit one purpose. We cannot expect Integration Governance to be enforced by people whose immediate interest is not related with integrations. No hard feelings, but how it would look like if one Product Team would be designing Business Processes in the other Product?

Integration design for “reusable” Business APIs, and ideally only Business APIs (as designing anything on the Provider system and Consumer experience is not their responsibility), has to be assigned to Integration Architects and Integration Team.

Conclusion

The main purpose of this article is to:

  • Demonstrate how easily we can apply API layers with SAP Integration Sute.
  • Explain what the key benefits are of “breaking” APIs into layers.
  • (Re)emphasize the need for proper Integration Governance and the role of Integration Architects.

This article is (obviously) not a technical article. Here are I am discussing about a concept which may further enhance our Decoupled/Lousily-coupled integrations – and make our journey toward Composable Business/Application Architecture and Intelligence Enterprise much easier.

It is for each Organization/Enterprise to evaluate what is their path. General view is, complex Organizations/Enterprises with very heterogenic environments might see numerous benefits in bringing additional governance with API layers, in their organizational schema.

But, as always (in IT), one size does not fit all…

Acknowledgment

*) Intro photo by Conny Schneider on Unsplash

References

[1] API Handyman Blog: API Handyman | Organizing APIs in System, Business, and Experience Layers

[2] API Handyman Blog: API Handyman | Organize APIs around business domains and capabilities, not tools

[3] Medium Blog: API-Led Principles: What’s Permitted and What’s Not | Medium

[4] LinkedIn Article: Distinction between system-, process and experience APIs

[5] MuleSoft: Types of APIs and how to determine which to build | Mulesoft

[6] SAP: Integration Solution Advisory Methodology (ISA-M):... - SAP Community

entry.title: How to Set Up B2B Outbound Integration with Trading Partner Management in SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
1. Introduction

If you work in integration long enough, you hear the same prediction again and again, EDI is going away. In reality, that has not happened. APIs and events have absolutely changed the landscape, but in many B2B scenarios, EDI is still very much alive. For a lot of companies, it remains one of the most important ways to exchange business documents with partners.

SAP’s B2B integration story has also changed quite a bit over the years. Many teams started with Seeburger on SAP XI/PI, then moved to the SAP B2B add-on on PO. In the cloud world, SAP Cloud Integration has gone through a few stages as well from basic converters, to Integration Advisor with MIGs and MAGs, and now to Trading Partner Management (TPM) together with B2B monitoring.

Because of that evolution, TPM can feel harder than it really is, especially when you first open it. It involved MIGs, agreements, identifiers, communication channels, templates, standard iFlows, custom mapping flows, and runtime processing. With so many changes over the years, it is understandable that EDI on Cloud Integration can seem complex at first.

That is exactly why I wanted to write this. Instead of starting with a complex multi-partner setup, I think it is much easier to begin with a simple outbound scenario and understand the core building blocks first. Once that mental model is clear, the rest of the setup becomes much more manageable.

In this post, I will focus on a basic outbound flow and walk through the main setup steps in SAP Integration Suite.

What “Outbound” means in TPM

In an outbound scenario, company initiates the message exchange:

  1. A business document is created in the backend, often as an IDoc from ERP or SAP S/4HANA.
  2. IDoc is sent to Cloud Integration.
  3. Cloud Integration transforms it into an EDI message, for example an X12 850
  4. Envelopes it into an interchange and delivers it to the trading partner.
  5. Optionally you can also support functional acknowledgements (for example, X12 997).

2.    End to End Setup Overview in Cloud Integration

When you look at TPM for the first time, one of the biggest questions is usually What needs to be done, and in what order?

For a basic outbound EDI setup, this is the sequence I typically follow:

  1. Create MIGs for IDoc, X12, or EDIFACT
  2. Create the required number ranges
  3. Deploy and configure the standard TPM iFlows
  4. Build the custom mapping iFlow
  5. Define your own company profile
    • identifiers
    • systems
    • communications
  6. Define the trading partner profile
    • identifiers
    • systems
    • communications
  7. Create agreement templates
  8. Create agreements and B2B scenarios
  9. Perform end-to-end testing

There are a lot of individual objects in that list, but once you work through them in sequence, the setup starts to make sense.

3.    Integration Advisor

Before you get into agreements, routing, or acknowledgements, you need the message structures in place. In SAP Integration Suite, that starts with Message Implementation Guidelines (MIGs).

3.1 Create MIGs for IDoc / X12 / EDIFACT

MIGs matter because they act as the structural definition for the messages TPM works with. They are used in validation, routing, and transaction configuration.

3.1.1  Steps to Create Message Implementation Guidelines (MIGs) for IDoc

To create an IDoc MIG:

  1. Navigate to Design → MIGs.

     

  2. Create a new MIG

     

  3. Choose the standard type system for SAP S/4HANA On Premise IDoc. Search for the message type you need, for example ORDERS.ORDERS05. Select the appropriate version, such as S/4HANA 1709. You can skip uploading a payload if you do not need it for this step.

     

     

     

     

  4. When naming the MIG, make sure the direction fits your use case:

    • IN = the MIG is used as the source
    • OUT = the MIG is used as the target
    • BOTH = the same MIG may be used in either direction

       

  5. Under Own Business Context, select the relevant Country/Region. Then click on Create.
    Examples:
    X12: United States
    EDIFACT: United Kingdom
    IDoc: United States and United Kingdom (as applicable)

     

     

  6. Right-click the node, select required fields, then Save and Activate.

     

     

3.1.2 Steps to Create Message Implementation Guidelines (MIGs) for X12

In this example, the outbound IDoc is mapped to X12 850 version 5010. That means you also need a second MIG that represents the X12 target structure.

The process is basically the same as the IDoc MIG creation. The main point is that both the source and target structures are defined clearly before you continue.

 

 

4 Cloud Integration

4.1   Deploy Standard Trading Partner Management Iflows

  1. Navigate to Discover → Integrations.

     

  2. Search for “Cloud Integration - Trading Partner Management V2”. Select the package, then click the Copy button in the top-right corner.

     

     

  3. Go to Design → Integration & APIs. Search for “Cloud Integration - Trading Partner Management V2”, select the package, and deploy the required integration flows.
  4. Depending on the connectivity setup, deploy the following standard content as-is (no changes):
    Reusable Groovy Scripts V2
    Step 1 - Sender AS2 Communication Flow V2
    Step 1 - Sender IDOC Communication Flow V2
    Step 1 - Sender Process Direct Communication Flow V2
    Step 1b - Write Message to Message Queue
    Step 2 - Interchange Processing Flow V2
    Step 3 - Receiver Communication Flow V2
    Note: Based on your connectivity model, only the relevant Step 1 sender flow needs to be deployed. However, the following are mandatory in all cases:
    • Reusable Groovy Scripts
    • Step 1b
    • Step 2
    • Step 3

    These iFlows provide the standard runtime pipeline used by TPM for routing, enveloping, and message delivery.

4.2 Create Custom Mapping Flow

In Cloud Integration, there are two main transformation approaches:

  • Message Mapping
  • Mapping Guidelines (MAG)

In this scenario, we use Message Mapping to convert data from IDoc to EDI and vice versa.

So the custom iFlow is responsible for transforming the IDoc into the target EDI structure. TPM then calls that iFlow at runtime through a Process Direct endpoint. When you configure the agreement later, you reference this custom mapping using a mapping exit.

A simple way to think about it is:

  • Standard TPM content = routing, interchange handling, delivery
  • Custom mapping iFlow = your document transformation logic

That separation helped me a lot when I was trying to understand where each piece belongs.

 

 

 

 

 

 

4.3  Create Number Ranges

Next, create the number ranges required for interchange or transaction control.

 

 

Create 5a number range and provide the following details:

  1. Name: Number range name as referenced in Trading Partner Management
  2. Description
  3. Minimum Value
  4. Maximum Value
  5. Field Length: If set to 0, the system derives the length from the maximum value
  6. Rotate: Indicates whether numbering should restart from the minimum value after the maximum is reached

If you do not want a separate number range for every partner, you can also create default number ranges and reuse them across multiple agreements.

 

 

5. Trading Partner Management

5.1  Define “Self” Company Profile in Trading Partner Management

5.1.1 Create Company Profile

Create a new company profile for your own organization, for example SELF_COMPANY.

Note: Only one company profile can exist per tenant.

 

 

 

5.1.2  Create Identifiers (for Self)

You can maintain multiple identifiers for your self-company profile. In this example, we create two:

  • ERP Identifier– DR1CLNT100 (SNDPRN)
    • Identification: DR1CLNT100
    • Alias: DR1CLNT100
    • Type System: SAP S/4HANA On Premise IDoc
    • Scheme: N/A

       

  • EDI Identifier – SELF_1234
    • Identification: SELF_1234
    • Alias: SELF_1234
    • Type System: ASC X12
    • Scheme: ZZ

       

5.1.3 Create Systems (for Self)

Under the Systems tab, create the backend system definition.

The naming is flexible, but I would recommend keeping it consistent and easy to recognize, especially once you have multiple landscapes. 

In this example:

  • Name: S4
  • Alias: S4
  • Type: Create New in this case
    • Name: S4
    • Description: S4
    • Deployment Type: OnPremise
    • Application: S4
  • Purpose: Dev

     

     

     

5.1.3.1  Create Type Systems ( for Self)

Create the relevant type system, for example:

  • Name: SAP S/4HANA On Premise IDoc
  • Version: your SAP S/4HANA release

If you are not sure which version to use, the important thing is that it aligns with the DOCREL value in the IDoc control record EDI_DC40.

 

 

 

5.1.3.1  Create Communications

Since SAP S/4HANA is sending the IDoc into Cloud Integration, create a sender communication channel such as:

  • Name: SND_IDOC
  • Alias: SND_IDOC
  • Direction: Sender
  • Adapter: IDOC

     

5.2  Define Trading Partner in Trading Partner Management

After your self-company is ready, create the external partner profile.

5.2.1  Create Trading Partners

Go to Design → B2B Scenarios. Under the Partner Profile tab, choose Create Profile.

Example:

  • Partner Name: PARTNER1
  • Company Short Name: PARTNER1

     

     

5.2.2 Create Identifiers (for Trading Partner)

You can maintain multiple identifiers for each trading partner. In this example, we create two:

  • ERP Identifier– PARTNER1(RCVPRN)
    • Identification: PARTNER1
    • Alias: PARTNER1
    • Type System: SAP S/4HANA On Premise IDoc
    • Scheme: N/A

       

  • EDI Identifier – PART_123
    • Identification: PART_123
    • Alias: PART_123
    • Type System: ASC X12
    • Scheme: ZZ

       

5.2.3  Create System (for Trading Partner)

Create a system entry for the trading partner. This represents the external side of the exchange and is later connected to the communication details.

Create a System for Trading Partner for PARTNER1.

 

 

 

 

5.2.3.1  Create Type System (for Trading Partner)

Create a Type System for the Trading Partner with the Below details

  • Name: ASC X12
  • Type: 005010

     

5.2.3.2   Create Communications (for Trading Partner)

Since the trading partner receives the outbound EDI from Cloud Integration, create a receiver communication channel.

  • Name: RCV_PD
  • Alias: RCV_PD
  • Direction: Receiver
  • Adapter: ProcessDirect
  • Address: /PD

     

5.3 Agreement Template

Agreement templates are optional, but they become very useful once you start scaling.

If you only have one partner and one transaction, they may feel like extra setup. But if you expect multiple partners or multiple message types, templates help you standardize the overall agreement structure and reduce repeated configuration.

 

 

 

 

5.4 Agreements

Agreements are where the relationship between your company, the partner, and the business transaction is actually defined.

To create one:

  • Go to the Agreement tab and click on create.

     

  • Select the agreement template, for example SAP_PARTNER

     

  • Choose Copy from Template

     

  • Select the trading partner

     

  • Open the draft agreement

     

This is the point where TPM starts to feel much more concrete, because now you are tying the profiles, message structures, mappings, and communication setup together.

5.4.1 B2B Scenarios

Inside the agreement, create the actual B2B scenario.

Recommended example:

  • Name: PARTNER1_ORDERS_ORDERS05_To_850_5010
  • Business Transaction Pattern: One way notification
  • For an outbound scenario, Set your company role to Initiator (For an inbound scenario, Set your company role to Reactor) 

     

     

After creating the transaction:

  1. Select the Sender Communication Channel

     

  2. Select the Sender Interchange and assign the appropriate MIG

     

  3. Maintain the Mapping Process Direct adapter address

     

  4. Configure the Receiver Interchange
    • Select the appropriate MIG
    • Assign the number range
    • Enable functional acknowledgement if required
    • Maintain custom separators if needed

       

  5. Enter the Receiver Communication Channel

Repeat the same process for any additional transactions required for other message types or partners.

Finally, save and activate the agreement.

 

 

In my view, this is the most important part to understand conceptually: the agreement is where TPM becomes executable. Until the agreement is active, the rest of the setup is mostly just preparation.

5.5 Perform End To End Testing

Once the following setups are in place, you are ready for your first end to end test:

  • MIGs are created and activated
  • Standard TPM iFlows are deployed
  • Self company and trading partner profiles are maintained
  • Agreement is configured and activated
  • Custom mapping iFlow is available through ProcessDirect

At this point, you can send a test IDoc from the backend, let Cloud Integration process and transform it into EDI, and verify that the message is successfully routed and delivered to the trading partner.

 

 

 

Final thoughts

When I first started working with Trading Partner Management, it seemed more complex than I expected. There are many setups involved, and at the beginning it is not always obvious what is essential and what can wait. But once I worked through a simple outbound scenario, the overall design started to make much more sense.

What helped me most was thinking about TPM in three parts:

  • message structure with MIGs
  • runtime orchestration with the standard TPM flows
  • business transformation with the custom mapping iFlow

Once those three pieces are clear, TPM becomes much easier to approach.

If you are just getting started, I would recommend building one simple outbound scenario end to end before moving on to multiple partners, multiple message types, or reusable templates. That first working flow gives you a solid foundation for the rest of the setup.

entry.title: Certification Program for Partner-built AI Agents with Joule Studio
entry.content:
Accelerate Your AI Agent's Market Success: launch offer for Certification of partner-built agents

Overview:

Enterprise AI has moved from aspiration to operational necessity. Yet achieving AI that is production-ready, contextually grounded, and enterprise-safe has traditionally demanded integrating multiple environments, tools, and runtimes — creating complexity that slows delivery and increases risk. SAP addresses this directly with the SAP Business AI Platform - a single, unified platform to build, contextualize, and govern AI applications and workflows across the enterprise.

At the core of this platform is Joule Studio — an AI-first development environment designed for SAP partners. With Joule Studio, partners can design, build, and extend AI agents powered by SAP Business AI Platform, bringing deep industry and domain expertise directly into SAP's ecosystem. These agents go beyond natural language understanding: they automate complex workflows, access real-time APIs, execute calculations and scripts, interface with third-party systems, and integrate natively with SAP solutions — all without additional operational overhead.

Launching certification program for partner-built Joule Agents:

SAP Integration and Certification Center (SAP ICC), as part of SAP Partner Innovation Lifecycle Services (PILS), has launched a dedicated certification program for partner-built Joule agents. This program validates AI-powered agents that integrate with the SAP Business AI Platform against SAP's highest technical and compliance standards — and gives partners a clear, structured path to earn the official SAP Certified designation.

As the ecosystem of partner-built Joule agents grows, SAP ICC is committed to providing the framework partners need to bring trusted, high-quality agents to market with confidence.

The certification program covers technical and integration criteria, offering a comprehensive scope to ensure that your agents meet the necessary requirements in 5 key dimensions.

  • Development best practices — Adhere to SAP's published guidelines for building custom Joule agents as documented on SAP Help Portal.
  • Agent provisioning and deployment — Meet SAP's approved provisioning and deployment approach for Joule agents.
  • SAP API Policy adherence — Any integration with SAP solutions must adhere to the SAP API Policy and use endorsed integration pathways. [reference: API Policy FAQ , SAP Reference Architecture]
  • SAP AI Ethics policy confirmation — alignment with SAP's AI Ethics policies for AI development and deployment.
  • End-to-end functional integration test — A functional test to ensure the agent perform the action as per their defined business scope.

What Certification delivers for Your Business

Achieving SAP certification is more than a technical milestone — it is a competitive differentiator.

  • Accelerated Market Success: Certification serves as a proof point that drives faster sales cycles, accelerates customer deployment in SAP landscapes, and increases customer confidence.
  • Global Visibility: Get listed in the SAP Certified Solutions Directory and position the agent as 'certified' by SAP to prospective customers.
  • Official SAP Certified Designation:  You will be awarded with SAP Certified – Built with SAP Business AI logo and certificate, demonstrating technical alignment with SAP solutions

          

  • Spotlight+ Designation Eligibility: The partner-built Joule agent certification is an eligible scenario for Spotlight+ — SAP's earned designation for strategically aligned certified, high-performing solutions within SAP’s partner solution progression framework. With immediate effect, solutions certified under this program that also meet the SAP Store sales performance criteria can qualify for Spotlight+ in the next quarterly evaluation, unlocking preferred placement on SAP Store, go-to-market acceleration, access to Development Funds, and packaged demand generation campaigns.

Launch Offer: Free Certification Through End of 2026*

To accelerate partner adoption of the SAP Business AI Platform, SAP ICC is offering launch offer of free certification for qualified partner-built agents — available on a limited basis through the end of 2026.

This offer reflects SAP ICC's commitment to supporting partners in building innovative, trustworthy AI agents that meet the needs of enterprise customers.

Next Steps

To begin the certification process, discuss technical feasibility, or learn more about the program, contact SAP ICC directly:

Email: icc@sap.com | Order via: Partner Benefits Catalog

Secure your slot and demonstrate to your customers that your agent meets SAP's highest standards — before availability closes.

This is a blog post by the SAP Integration and Certification Center. For more information on our services and certifications, visit our website.
